coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence sensor for the detection of zearalenone based on magnetic molecular imprinted polymers and carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yu-Fan
Hu, Xiao-Peng
Luo, Qing-Qing
Liao, Song
Gu, Hui-Wen
Yan, Xiu-Fan
Yin, Xiao-Li
coredata.dc:description: Zearalenone (ZEN) poses a threat by infiltrating the food chain and triggering various diseases; hence, the precise detection of ZEN is essential for ensuring food safety. Herein, a fluorescence sensor was constructed for sensitive and selective detection of ZEN based on magnetic molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs/Fe3O4@SiO2) and carbon dots (CDs). MIPs/Fe3O4@SiO2, serving as recognition elements, captured ZEN in real samples. Using a magnet, we separated the MIPs/Fe3O4@SiO2 that had captured ZEN and added them to a specific volume of the CDs solution, resulting in fluorescence quenching of the CDs. As the CDs were not directly exposed to the sample solution, these fluorescence probes exhibit a satisfactory level of anti-interference performance. Under optimal experimental conditions, an increase in ZEN concentration results in a linear decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. The sensor has a linear response range of 0.5–30 ng mL−1 with the limit of detection as low as 0.14 ng mL−1 (LOD = 3 σ/s). Furthermore, this developed sensor demonstrated high selectivity, sensitivity, and stability. This sensor has good application potential in the analysis of ZEN in complicated samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zearalenone
Magnetic molecular imprinted polymers
Fluorescence sensing
Carbon dots

Feasibility of molecular imprinted fluorescence sensing platform

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of Fe3O4@SiO2 (A), MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 (B); TEM image of MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 (C); TEM image of CDs (D); The corresponding particle size distribution analysis of CDs (E); FT-IR spectra before (black line) and after (red line) adsorption of ZEN (F).

Fluorescence emission spectra of MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 (A) and NIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 (B) combined with ZEN; (C) Evaluation diagrams of matrix effects of fluorescence sensor. The fluorescence intensity of CDs in different volumes (0 μL, 100 μL, 400 μL, 800 μL) of corn flour extracts with or without ZEN (30 ng mL−1); (D) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs in different systems; (E) Effect of the presence of ZEN (30 ng mL−1) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs in different volumes (0 μL, 100 μL, 400 μL, 800 μL) of corn flour extracts; (F) Fluorescence intensity of CDs in different systems (MIP stands for MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2; extract stands for corn flour extract).
(A) The volume ratio of APTES and TEOS; (B) The amount ratio of MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 and CDs; (C) Incubation time; (D) Incubation and detection pH value.

Fluorescence spectra of anti-matrix effect molecular imprinting fluorescence sensors in PBS (A, pH=7) and in corn flour extract (C), respectively; Linear relationships between F530 and ZEN concentration in PBS (B, pH=7) and corn flour extract (D), respectively (n = 3).

(A) Responses of the MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 and NIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 to different species (IF (Imprinting Factor) = ΔFMIP-530/ΔFNIP-530); (B) Influence of interferents on the response of ZEN; (C) Stability of the fluorescence sensor; (D) The response values of ten measurements by using one sensor; (E) The responses of ten sensors.

(A) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs aqueous suspension in the absence and presence of ZEN (5 ng mL−1); (B) UV absorption spectrum of ZEN and emission spectrum of CDs; (C) Effect of temperature on the Ksv of the Stern-Volmer curve.
Construction and detection principle of the fluorescence sensor. (A) Synthesis of MIP/ Fe3O4@SiO2; (B) Synthesis of CDs; (C) Fluorescence determination of ZEN.
Comparison of the construction strategy and sensing performance with the reported fluorescence sensors for ZEN detection.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence sensor for the detection of zearalenone based on magnetic molecular imprinted polymers and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Sciences, College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Sciences, College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Tobacco, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Tobacco, Guizhou University, Guiyang, 550025

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zearalenone (ZEN) poses a threat by infiltrating the food chain and triggering various diseases; hence, the precise detection of ZEN is essential for ensuring food safety. Herein, a fluorescence sensor was constructed for sensitive and selective detection of ZEN based on magnetic molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs/Fe3O4@SiO2) and carbon dots (CDs). MIPs/Fe3O4@SiO2, serving as recognition elements, captured ZEN in real samples. Using a magnet, we separated the MIPs/Fe3O4@SiO2 that had captured ZEN and added them to a specific volume of the CDs solution, resulting in fluorescence quenching of the CDs. As the CDs were not directly exposed to the sample solution, these fluorescence probes exhibit a satisfactory level of anti-interference performance. Under optimal experimental conditions, an increase in ZEN concentration results in a linear decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. The sensor has a linear response range of 0.5–30 ng mL−1 with the limit of detection as low as 0.14 ng mL−1 (LOD = 3 σ/s). Furthermore, this developed sensor demonstrated high selectivity, sensitivity, and stability. This sensor has good application potential in the analysis of ZEN in complicated samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Zearalenone (ZEN), a pervasive mycotoxin, is a significant menace to the contemporary food sector and animal husbandry (Luo et al., 2019; Niazi et al., 2022). Ingesting food contaminated with ZEN can result in severe health conditions (Ji et al., 2019). ZEN is classified as a class III carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (Hu et al., 2020). Given the toxicity of ZEN and its potential risks to humans, monitoring ZEN is very important in food. Primary methods for detecting ZEN in China include instrumental detection and immunoassays. Instrumental methods such as liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Kumar et al., 2022; Murtaza et al., 2023) and matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (Vidal-Acuña et al., 2018) offer high sensitivity and accuracy but suffer from high cost, complexity, and long detection duration. While providing speed and convenience, immunoassays often utilize antibodies as recognition elements, rendering them expensive, time-consuming, and prone to false positives (Kou et al., 2021). However, fluorescence sensors have seen rapid advancements, attributed to their rapid detection capabilities, high sensitivity, and ease of operation. In parallel, molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs), renowned for their exceptional stability and selectivity, have garnered significant interest among researchers. Therefore, the development of a fluorescence sensor leveraging MIPs for the detection of zearalenone (ZEN) holds substantial significance. The efficacy of molecular imprinted fluorescent sensors depends on fluorescent materials. Carbon dots (CDS) stand out because of their remarkable fluorescence properties, environmental friendliness, good biocompatibility, high stability, and low-cost synthesis strategies (Liu et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2021). Molecular imprinted fluorescence sensors, integrated with CDs, have been engineered for the detection of pollutants in real samples (Hu et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022). For example, Liu et al.(Liu et al., 2024) successfully designed an innovative molecular imprinted polymer dual-emission fluorescence sensor (CDs@PDA-MIPs) strategy for the sensitive detection of Nifedipine (NIF). The fluorescence intensity of the probe decreased with increasing NIF concentration and showed a good linear relationship. Therefore, we believed that CDs were employed as the fluorescence probes in molecular imprinted fluorescence sensors for sensitive detection of ZEN.

To date, matrix interference remains a significant challenge in the application of fluorescence sensors. Moreover, the nonspecific adsorption of MIPs can exacerbate the impact of matrix interference, thereby affecting the performance of these sensors. To address these issues, various antifouling materials have been developed (e.g. polyethylene glycol, acrylamide polymers, zwitterionic, etc.) (Hu et al., 2022). These materials, known for their exceptional hydrophilicity, are extensively utilized in biosensors to improve selectivity. However, fixing these antifouling materials onto MIPs requires tedious steps and demanding conditions. Consequently, there is an urgent need to develop a molecular imprinted fluorescence sensor that delivers superior analytical performance and selectivity in real samples.

In this study, a CDs-based magnetic molecular imprinted fluorescence sensor was developed for sensitive and selective detection of ZEN. The construction and detection principle of the proposed sensor is illustrated in Scheme 1. Firstly, magnetic MIPs (MIPs/Fe3O4@SiO2) were utilized to capture ZEN in samples. Subsequently, MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 were isolated using a magnet and introduced into a CDs solution. Notably, this proposed sensor demonstrated satisfactory sensitivity. This sensor not only holds promise for practical applications in detecting ZEN in real samples but also offers a potential approach for the detection of other mycotoxins.

FeCl₃·6H₂O was purchased from Sigma (USA). Anhydrous sodium acetate (NaAc), ochratoxin A (OTA), KCl, CuCl2, Zn(NO3)2, and citric acid monohydrate have been sourced from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Company (China). Quercetin, 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), ethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), polyethyleneimine (MW=600), ZEN, fumonisin B1 (FB1), and aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) were supplied from Macklin Reagent Co (Shanghai, China). Ammonia and isopropanol were bought from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co. LTD (China). Methanol, ethanol, and ethylene glycol were bought from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. LTD (Shanghai, China). ZEN ELISA kit was purchased from Northern Weiye Metrology Group Co., LTD (Beijing, China). ZEN standard solutions were prepared with ethanol and diluted. Went to a local supermarket to buy corn flour, wheat flour, oil samples, and corn juice.

Using a Hitachi S4800 scanning electron microscopy (Hitachi, Japan) to take Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images. Determined CDs’ morphology through scanning transmission electron microscopy (TEM; HT-7800, 100 kV, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) displayed fluorescence spectra. UV absorption spectra were analyzed using a TU-1900 spectrophotometer (Beijing Puji General Co., China).

Fe3O4@SiO2 composite was synthesized according to the following procedure (Feng et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021a, p. 71): A solution was prepared by dissolving 1.081 g of FeCl₃·6H₂O and 3.691 g of NaAc in 60 mL of ethylene glycol, followed by stirring for 0.5 h. The mixture was then transferred to a PTFE-lined high-pressure reactor and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After the high-pressure reactor cooled to room temperature, it was collected using magnets and repeatedly cleaned with ultra-pure water and ethanol. Finally, Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dried for 12 h at 50 °C. In a subsequent step, 500 mg Fe3O4 nanoparticles, 100 mL isopropanol, and 4 mL ultra-pure water were mixed and sonicated for 20 min. After that, 2 mL TEOS and 5 mL ammonia were added to the solution and it was agitated for 6 h at 45 °C. The resulting precipitate was isolated using magnets and washed extensively with ethanol and ultra-pure water. After that, the residues (4 g) were dissolved in 100 mL of absolute ethanol. Then 2 mL APTES and 60 mL ammonia were added into the suspension and stirred for 12 h. Multiple washings and 60 °C drying were applied to the Fe3O4@SiO2 obtained.

MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 was synthesized following previously reported methods (Lu et al., 2023). 80 mg of quercetin and 2.8 mL of APTES were added to 48 mL of ethanol and stirred the mixture for 20 min. Next, added TEOS (8 mL), Fe3O4@SiO2 (375 mg), and ammonia (32 mL) successively to the solution above and stirred for 12 h. The product obtained was subsequently eluted with a methanol-acetic acid mixture (3:2, v/v) for 6 h, and denoted as MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2. Notably, the synthesis of NIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 was the same as that of MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2. The only difference was that no quercetin was added.

The CDs were created using a one-step hydrothermal method (Cui et al., 2022). A mixture was prepared by combining 0.6 g of polyethyleneimine with 0.6 g of citric acid monohydrate in 10 mL of ultrapure water, which was then sonicated to achieve a homogeneous solution. This solution was subsequently transferred to a hydrothermal reactor and maintained at 180 °C for a duration of 2 h to facilitate the reaction. Finally, the prepared CDs solution was subjected to centrifugation and filtration, and the purified CDs were stored at 4 °C.

The relative fluorescent quantum yield of the prepared CDs was determined (Zhu et al., 2015). In this process, sulfuric acid (0.1 mol L−1) was used as a solvent, and quinine sulfate (QY=54 %) was chosen as a fluorescence standard solution. The absolute quantum yield value of the CDs was determined by referencing the constant fluorescence quantum yield of the quinine sulfate standard. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was calculated according to the method proposed by Zhang et al. (Han et al., 2018) with a fluorescent signal recorded at the excitation wavelength of 485 nm.

The appropriate amount of MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 (200 μL, 10 mg mL−1) was added into a phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.01 M, 1300 μL, pH = 7.0) containing different concentrations of ZEN, and the solution was shaken for 5 min. Then the MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 was separated with a magnet and transferred to PBS (1200 μL, 0.01 M, pH = 7.0) containing CDs (800 μL). Finally, the fluorescence spectra of this mixture were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, with the excitation wavelength set at 485 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ZEN in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of corn and wheat samples for analysis involved the following (Lin et al., 2023). Initially, the samples were dosed with varying concentrations of a standard ZEN solution (0, 3, 12, 28 ng mL−1). Subsequently, a 5.0 g sample was added to 25 mL methanol-water (7: 3, v/v) solution. The resulting mixture was then subjected to filtration through a 0.22 μm membrane to separate the filtrate. The ensuing transparent sample solution was carefully collected and set aside for subsequent analysis.

For oil samples and corn juice (Chi and Liu, 2023), the ZEN standard solutions have been transferred to them (5 g). Next, aqueous methanol (25 mL, 7:3, v/v), and after oscillating for 0.5 h, the mixtures were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant from each sample was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to achieve clarity. The filtrate was subsequently evaporated to remove the solvent, leaving behind a residue. At last, the collected residue was redissolved in PBS (0.01 M, pH=7).
Contaminated samples are treated following the actual sample approach above.

The matrix effects (ME) equation is as follows:
(1)
ME
%
=
M
B
M
A
× 100 %
Where MB is the CDs fluorescence intensity when ZEN is present in the corn flour extract and MA is the fluorescence intensity emitted by CDs when exposed to ZEN in PBS. ME < 0.8, indicating a matrix-suppressing effect; 0.80 < ME ≤ 1.20, indicating a weak matrix effect; 1.2 < ME ≤ 1.5, indicating a strong matrix effect.

The morphology of Fe3O4@SiO2 and MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 have been investigated by using SEM. In Fig. 1 A, Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles had an average particle size of approximately 200 nm, with a distinct SiO2 layer observable on their surface. In contrast, the particle size of MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 was notably larger, as evidenced in Fig. 1 B, due to the additional coating of the MIPs film on the Fe3O4@SiO2 core. The morphology of Fe3O4@SiO2 and MIP/ Fe3O4@SiO2 were also studied by using TEM. The SiO2 layer can be seen clearly (Fig S1). Compared with Fe3O4@SiO2, the particle size of MIP/ Fe3O4@SiO2 was larger (Fig. 1 C). These observations confirm the successful synthesis of the MIP. The CDs morphology was studied by using TEM (Fig. 1 D). The CDs possessed regular shapes with a size of approximately 2.5 nm (Fig. 1 E). Moreover, the relative QY of the CDs was determined to be approximately 58.03 %.

The MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles were further characterized using FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 1 F, the absorption peak at 1548 cm−1 is attributed to the N-H bond of APTES, a signature feature of the compound (Fu et al., 2020). The peak at 2927 cm−1 was the stretch vibration peak of C-H, confirming the successful formation of the MIPs layer on the Fe3O4@SiO2 nanospheres (Fu et al., 2020). Additionally, the peak at 1517 cm−1, associated with the stretching vibration of the benzene ring, suggests that the MIPs have effectively bound to ZEN (Piovesana et al., 2020). Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was used to investigate further the relative composition of the magnetic core and organic shell in the Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO, and MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 particles. The results are shown in Fig. S2. The Fe3O4 curve indicated a 7.02 % weight loss, primarily due to the evaporation of residual water and moisture. Above 200 °C, the organic shell gradually lost weight and usually decomposed completely at 400 °C. The curve of Fe3O4@SiO2 showed a weight loss of 1.11 % in terms of Fe3O4, indicating that the SiO2 on the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2 gradually lost weight. In contrast, the MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 curve exhibited a 3.04 % weight loss relative to Fe3O4@SiO2, which is indicative of the MIPs layer gradually shedding mass.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of molecular imprinted fluorescence sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the affinity of MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 to ZEN, the capabilities of MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 and NIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 have been studied. As shown in Fig. 2 A, a significant reduction in the fluorescence intensity of CDs following the incubation of MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 in ZEN solution, indicative of ZEN being effectively captured by the MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2. Under the identical experimental condition, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained relatively unchanged when NIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 was used (Fig. 2 B), due to the absence of specific binding sites for ZEN. These findings confirm that the MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 composite was successfully synthesized and possesses the anticipated molecular recognition properties.

The selectivity of the sensor was thoroughly evaluated. As shown in Figs. 2 C and 2 D, in the presence of ZEN, the fluorescence of CDs is quenched, while the fluorescence of CDs is unchanged when ZEN is absent. As shown in Fig. 2 E, the ME of the corn flour extract (100 μL, 400 μL, and 800 μL) were 100.2 %, 98.9 %, and 99.9 %, respectively, which belonged to the weak ME. These values indicate a weak matrix effect, suggesting that the sensor is not significantly influenced by the matrix components. Notably, in Fig. 2 F, the fluorescence of the CDs was quenched upon direct contact with the corn meal extract, but it remained stable after MIP adsorption and magnetic separation. As shown in Fig. S6, wheat flour, oil, and corn juice had the same results. The above experimental results show that the sensor has good selectivity.

The experimental conditions have been optimized ( Fig. 3). The volume ratio of APTES to TEOS was studied. As shown in Fig. 3 A, a volume ratio of 7:20 between APTES to TEOS gave the best results. Next, the amount of MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 was also investigated (Fig. 3 B). 300 μL MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 (2 mg mL−1) were chosen to obtain the best results. At this time, the imprinting effect is the best, and the fluorescence quenching degree of CDs is the best. Additionally, we fine-tuned the incubation time, as well as the pH levels for both incubation and detection. As shown in Fig. 3 C and 3D, the optimal incubation time was 20 min, and the optimal incubation and detection pH values were both 7.0. (Quenching percentage: F0 - F/ F0, where F0 is the CDs fluorescence without ZEN, F is the CDs fluorescence in ZEN).

Fig. 4A shows the fluorescence response of the proposed sensor to ZEN under optimal conditions. With the increase of ZEN, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 530 nm was progressively quenched. As shown in Fig. 4 B, there was a linear correlation between CDs fluorescence intensity and ZEN concentration (0.5–30 ng mL−1) and the equation was F0 - F = 9.69 CZEN + 185.45 (R
2 = 0.998). The LOD was 0.14 ng mL−1 (LOD =3 σ/s) and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) was 0.47 ng mL−1.

Then MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 was incubated in a corn sample extract containing ZEN, and the CDs fluorescence intensity was also examined. The linear relationship between ZEN concentration (from 1 to 30 ng mL−1) and the fluorescence intensity of CDs in Figs. 4 C and 4 D. As the concentration of ZEN increases, the CDs fluorescence decreases. Their linear relationship can be expressed as F0 - F = 7.31 CZEN + 200.66 (R
2 = 0.997). The LOD was 0.28 ng mL−1 (LOD = 3 σ/s) and the LOQ was 0.93 ng mL−1. By comparing the two detection results, this sensor demonstrates satisfactory anti-interference performance.

A comparison between this fluorescence sensor and other reported work was presented in Table 1 (Chi and Liu, 2023; C. Li et al., 2024; K. Li et al., 2024; Liao et al., 2023; Na et al., 2023; Santos et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2023). The proposed sensor in this work showed preferable sensitivity and repeatability as well as anti-interference performance.

The selectivity of the sensor was investigated by detecting ZEN, AFB1, OTA, FB1, AFG1, AFM1, and some ions. As shown in Fig. 5 A, after MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 was incubated in ZEN solution, the CDs fluorescence was quenched. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs remained largely unaffected when MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 was incubated with other solutions, highlighting the sensor's high specificity for ZEN.

The imprinting factor (IF) and selective factor (SF) were employed to quantify the impact of CDs on ZEN detection. According to the formula, IF = FMIP/ FNIP (Wang et al., 2021b), where FMIP is the fluorescence of CDs after incubation of MIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 in ZEN and analog solution; and FNIP is the fluorescence of CDs after incubation of NIP/Fe3O4@SiO2 in ZEN and analog solution. Fig. 5 A shows that the IF of ZEN is 4.45, a value exceeding that of other substances tested. Next, AFB1, OTA, and FB1, AFG1, AFM1 were used to further investigate the selectivity, and SF = IFZEN/IFanalog (Wang et al., 2021b), where IFZEN is ZEN’s IF; IFanalog is analog’s IF. The SF of OTA, AFB1, and FB1 are 4.42, 4.38, 3.99, 4.41, and 4.98 respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 5 B, when ZEN was added, there was a substantial fluorescence quenching at 530 nm. At the same time, the fluorescence of CDs remained substantially constant even when other competitors were present, which indicated that the sensor had significant anti-interference and recognition ability to the templates.

The stability of the sensors was also studied after being stored at 4 ℃ for 10 days (Fig. 5 C). The fluorescence intensity of the CDs remained largely unchanged, indicating good stability of the fluorescence sensor. Repeatability and reproducibility are crucial indicators of sensor performance. In the repetitive experiment, the same sensor was used to conduct ten consecutive measurements. As shown in Fig. 5 D, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained nearly unchanged, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) = 3.1 % (n = 10). Next, the reproducibility was assessed by detecting ZEN using 10 sensors. In Fig. 5 E, the performance of the ten sensors was almost consistent, and RSD = 2.7 % (n = 10). Collectively, these findings demonstrate the sensor's commendable repeatability and reproducibility.

For metal detection based on CDs, fluorescence quenching stands out as a prevalent sensing mechanism. This phenomenon can generally be divided into two distinct categories: static quenching, which involves the formation of ground-state complexes, and dynamic quenching, which stems from molecular collisions. The differentiation between these two types of quenching can be effectively achieved by examining fluorescence lifetime measurements or by considering the influence of temperature on the quenching process (Fu et al., 2022; Zu et al., 2017).

The fluorescence decay curves of CDs were demonstrated in Fig. 6 A. The fluorescence lifetime of the CDs was measured to be 3.03 ns in the absence of ZEN and extended to 3.67 ns when ZEN was present, indicating only a slight change. This minimal variation in lifetime suggests that the quenching mechanism is static (Callis, 2014). No overlap was found between the spectra of CDs and ZEN, which precludes the fluorescence resonance energy transfer quenching (Fig. 6 B). Next, the effect of temperature on the Stern-Volmer curve was studied. The equation is as follows:
(2)
F
0
F
= 1 + Ksv [ Q ]
where F0 is the fluorescence of CDs, F is the fluorescence of CDs in the presence of ZEN, KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, and Q is the equilibrium concentration of ZEN. Higher temperatures typically dissociated complexes with weak binding, resulting in static quenching (Steiner, 1984). Fig. 6 C illustrates a reduction in Ksv as the temperature increases, showing the presence of static quenching.

The proposed sensing platform was utilized for the quantitative analysis of ZEN in actual samples (i.e. corn, wheat, oil, and corn juice). After pretreating the samples, they were analyzed ( Table 2), and the results were consistent with those of the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). The rate of recovery varied between 93.00 % and 108.33 %, and RSD values ranged from 0.77 % to 4.4 %. These findings demonstrate the outstanding potential of the sensor for the accurate detection of ZEN in real samples.

The actual contaminated samples: corn flour, wheat flour, and corn juice were detected by ELISA, and the ZEN content was 109.6, 138.5, and 49.68 ng mL−1, respectively. The sensor was then employed to analyze the extracts of moldy samples. The results were consistent with the ELISA results ( Table 3).

In conclusion, we have successfully developed a molecular imprinted fluorescence sensor capable of detecting ZEN with both precision and sensitivity. MIP/ Fe3O4@SiO2, as the recognition element demonstrated excellent selectivity, while the signal probes CDs did not come into contact with the actual sample, which made the sensor have good resistance to matrix effects. The linear range of the approach was 0.5–30 ng mL−1, and the LOD was 0.14 ng mL−1. The method was further used for the detection of ZEN in real complex samples, and the recovery rate was from 93.00 % to 108.33 %. This sensing platform not only provides an accurate and sensitive method for ZEN detection in real samples but also lays the groundwork for the detection of other mycotoxins. In the future, the sensor is expected to be combined with machine learning algorithms to predict contamination trends and improve quality control processes.

Hui-Wen Gu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xiu-Fan Yan: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xiao-Li Yin: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Yu-Fan Yang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Investigation, Conceptualization. Xiao-Peng Hu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology. Qing-Qing Luo: Software, Formal analysis. Song Liao: Software, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their gratitude for the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 32272409, 32371501, and 22367004) and the Hubei Provincial Department of Education Science Research Program Young Talents Project (Q20231304).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2024.107185.
